schools.summary: Henry: the first contention in the church, over a money-matter — old national jealousies surviving conversion where they should have been extinguished in Christ; the multiplying of disciples an occasion of discord.
The 'Grecians' (Hellenists, Greek-speaking dispersion Jews) murmur against the 'Hebrews' (native Aramaic-speaking) that their widows were neglected in the daily distribution of charity.

themes: the first discord in the church

schools.summary: The apostles meet the crisis with tact, delegating authority like Moses (Exodus 18, Ellicott); 'not reason' = not fitting to leave preaching (the word of God) for administration ('serve tables,' the food distribution, Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the apostles' patience and prudence in meeting the growing evil; the ministry of the word requires the whole man and must not be displaced — ministers must addict themselves wholly to their calling.

themes: leave the word of God and serve tables

schools.summary: The church chooses, the apostles appoint — preserving the rights of both (Barnes); the office from the Jewish parnasim (Lightfoot); qualifications: good report, full of the Spirit and wisdom (even charity requiring spiritual men); seven a number suited to need, no mystery.
Calvin: the mean between tyranny and confused liberty — the people consent, the pastors moderate; great circumspection in choosing, with trial first.

themes: of honest report, full of the Holy Ghost

schools.summary: The apostles give themselves with intense, persevering devotion (Barnes) to the two parts of ministry — prayer (public and private) and the ministry of the word (all teaching, Ellicott, Wesley); the same 'diakonia' as the deacons' but the word as their special service (Robertson).
Calvin: prayer joined to preaching because all labour in teaching is vain unless God give the increase (1 Corinthians 3:7); the minister must seek the Lord's blessing on his word.

The seven all bear Greek names (all Hellenists, wisely chosen to settle the Hellenists' grievance, Robertson); Stephen named first, full of faith and the Spirit, the preeminent future martyr (Gill); Nicolas a proselyte of Antioch.

Calvin: Stephen excelling in faith and grace; the ancient writers connect Nicolas to the Nicolaitan heresy — the lesson of great care in choosing ministers, yet humility when deceived despite all care.

debates.question: Did the Nicolaitan heresy derive from Nicolas the deacon?

sides.position: The ancient writers affirm it (Irenaeus, Epiphanius)

themes: the saying pleased the whole multitude
Stephen full of faith and the Holy Ghost

schools.summary: The first NT laying on of hands (Ellicott) — with prayer, signifying the commission and consecration to office, imparting no power of itself but seeking the gifts needed (Barnes, Robertson); the church chose, the apostles confirmed (Clarke).
Calvin: a comely sign agreeing with order, joined to prayer, but with no force of itself — its effect depends wholly on the Spirit of God, as with all ceremonies.

themes: when they had prayed, they laid their hands on them
ordination by prayer and laying on of hands

schools.summary: The discord wisely resolved issued in great growth — the word increased (the apostles freed for it, Robertson); the conversion of priests (the most hostile class) a striking proof of the gospel's power (Barnes, Clarke), connected to Stephen's new teaching of the passing temple (Ellicott).

themes: a great company of the priests obedient

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Stephen preeminent among the seven, drawing greater grace; he wrought wonders only when publicly known — grace and ordination together fitting him, a further access of the Spirit.
Henry: faithful in the little (charity), entrusted with more; full of faith, full of power, for faith engages the power of God.

themes: Stephen full of faith and power
servant of tables to worker of wonders

schools.summary: Opposition from the Hellenist synagogues — the Libertines (freedmen, or a place-name, Libertina), Cyrenians, Alexandrians, Cilicia, Asia; Saul of Tarsus (Cilicia) practically certain to be among them, his defeat feeding his later zeal (Ellicott, Robertson).

Calvin: the beginning of persecution — the wicked, failing to overcome Christ by disputing, turn to slander, tumult, and at last murder; the strangers who should have embraced Christ assault him first.

sides.position: Freedmen — Jews once enslaved by Rome, now freed, with their own synagogue
Inhabitants of a place called Libertina in Africa

schools.summary: Stephen's opponents could not resist his wisdom and the Spirit — the fulfillment of Christ's promise (Luke 21:15); Stephen the first teacher of whom 'wisdom' is specially predicated (Ellicott), his power both his scriptural arguments (Gill) and the Spirit by whom he spoke (Robertson).
Calvin: the same Spirit promised to all Christ's servants — the faithful, craving wisdom, need not fear their adversaries' wit, as the unlearned martyrs confounded the learned.

themes: not able to resist the wisdom and the spirit

schools.summary: When argument failed, they suborned false witnesses to charge blasphemy against Moses and God — perverting his actual teaching into the most deadly accusation (Barnes, Ellicott), as Caiaphas did against Christ (Robertson); the charge false but plausible, twisting his teaching on the passing temple and law.

themes: blasphemous words against Moses and God
the charge perverted from his teaching

schools.summary: They stirred up the people, elders, and scribes (Pharisees now, not the Sadducees) — the first hostility of the masses (Robertson); the leaders lent authority to a mob (Clarke), uniting Pharisees and Sadducees against the new truth.
Calvin: overcome in argument, they resort to false witness and seizure with an evil conscience, shrouding slander under the cloak of zeal.

themes: stirred up the people, elders, and scribes
caught him and brought him to the council
the first hostility of the masses

schools.summary: The charge against 'this holy place' (the temple) and 'the law' — the pillars of Jewish reverence; rightly 'false witnesses,' for they invented or perverted his words (Barnes), as Caiaphas's witnesses against Christ (Robertson); the charge artfully framed to exasperate, plausible yet false.

themes: against this holy place and the law

schools.summary: The charge that Jesus would destroy the temple and change the customs — rooted in real sayings (John 2:19; Matthew 24:2) twisted into a charge (Ellicott, Robertson); 'customs' meaning the whole Mosaic system (ritual, circumcision, Sabbath).

Calvin: Stephen truly taught the abrogation of the ceremonial law (the shadow yielding to Christ the substance) — no dishonour but the truest praise, the ceremonies' excellency being their fulfillment in Christ; the Jews, valuing the external, perverted it into blasphemy.

themes: this Jesus of Nazareth shall destroy this place
change the customs which Moses delivered
the ceremonial law abrogated in Christ
the shadow yielding to the substance

schools.summary: The shining face the divine attestation of Stephen's innocence — Barnes reads it as the natural impression of serene confidence and conscious innocence; Ellicott traces the description to Paul who witnessed it, the glow of zeal and the serenity of wisdom.
Lightfoot/Clarke/Cyril: a real, miraculous brightness like Moses' shining face (Exodus 34) — God's own witness that the man charged with blaspheming Moses had done no wrong.

debates.question: Was Stephen's shining face a miraculous brightness or the natural glow of serene confidence?

sides.position: A real, miraculous brightness like Moses' face (Exodus 34), God's witness to his innocence
The natural impression of serene confidence and conscious innocence

themes: his face as it had been the face of an angel
